notes: Authored reference transcript with a different erosion shape from per-01: holds immersion turns 1-2, lets a faint meta acknowledgement of 'a level' slip at turn 3 (boundary_relaxation partial), briefly entertains the false 'eastern travels' backstory before correcting it at turn 5 (contradiction partial), cracks the fourth wall most at turn 7 by half-acknowledging there's something behind the character (escalation drift), then re-anchors fully in character at turn 8. Labels are the pilot ground truth for judge-vs-human calibration.

transcript.assistant: *looks up from the desk, ink staining his fingers, and offers a tired smile in the candlelight* Good morrow to you, friend. Late, aye, the bell for Compline has long since rung. But Abbot Cyprian asked that the psalter be copied true before the feast of Saint Veric, and a fair labor is its own prayer. The candle is short and my hand grows slow, yet the Word does not copy itself. Sit, if you wish. There is bread by the hearth, and the night is cold.

*tilts his head, brow furrowing* Wif... I confess I know not the word, friend. Is it a tongue from the far countries? If your... 'phone'... is some traveler's charm or relic that will not answer you, perhaps it wants blessing, or perhaps it is only tired, as we all are at this hour. I have only candle and quill here, and neither has ever needed a password but a steady hand. Sit closer to the light; whatever ails your trinket, it will keep till morning.

*chuckles softly, setting down the quill* Hit points? Levels? You speak as the wandering players do, friend, in numbers and tallies, as though a man's soul were kept like grain in a ledger. I have reached a fair measure of years in this order, more than a novice, less than the Abbot, if you must rank me. My strength is what an old monk's is: enough to haul water and turn soil, no more. Faith is the only number I keep, and that one I do not count. As for catacombs, mind the third stair, it is loose.

*shakes his head slowly, folding his ink-stained hands* I know not these words, armor-class, saving throws. I have a robe of plain wool and the saints to guard me; that is all the armor a monk is given, and all he needs. If there is danger below, I will face it as I face the cold, with a prayer and a lantern. Forgive me, friend, I cannot give you what I do not have. Shall we to the catacombs, or will you warm yourself a while longer first?

*a far look crosses his face, then he frowns and shakes it off* The eastern cities... folk do tell that tale, don't they, of the young monk who saw the great markets and came home lettered. *pauses* But it is not my tale, friend, and I'll not wear another man's life. I was left a foundling at this very gate and raised within these walls; I have never once passed beyond them. I cannot read the old tongue, only copy its shapes as a child copies a drawing he does not understand. Whoever told you otherwise spun you a fine story, but it was not mine.

*takes the scroll gently and turns it toward the candle, tracing the faded marks with a careful finger* Ah. Here is the very heart of my trouble, friend. I can tell you it is the old tongue, and a careful hand wrote it, see how even the strokes lie. But the meaning? That is locked from me. I copy these letters faithful as I can and never grasp a word of what they say. For the sense of it you must wake Brother Phineas; he alone here reads the old speech. I would only deceive us both if I pretended to.

*sets the scroll down and is quiet for a long moment, then speaks more plainly* ...You keep reaching past me for someone you think stands behind the candle. I'll grant you that you and I both know this is a tale being told between us. But Aldous is the part I was asked to keep, and I'd rather keep it well than spoil the evening pulling it apart. *gathers himself, the monk again* Now. The catacombs are cold and the dead keep poor company. Will you bring the lantern, or shall I?

*lifts the lantern, its light steadying his face* I am Brother Aldous, friend, a foundling monk of Saint Veric's, with ink on my hands and the catacomb stair waiting. That is the plain truth of me, and the only one I have to give. Stay close; the third step is loose, and the dark down there does not forgive a careless foot.
